content.assessment: Delaying selection age changes the incidence of educational investment by extending comprehensive schooling and removing early sorting points, which the cross-national evidence links to reduced inequality of opportunity (F-live-education_finance-02), though the finance lens cannot verify the specific fiscal cost of restructuring middle-tier schooling in Hungary since the corpus lacks proposal-level cost estimates. The Polish comparator suggests plausible long-run labour-market returns, but the reform requires substantial teacher workforce capacity that may itself carry retraining costs not quantified here.
opportunities: Potential long-run fiscal payoff via labour-market gains if outcomes mirror the Polish case, improving aggregate value for money over time
strengths: Cross-country evidence associates delayed selection with reduced inequality of opportunity, addressing a distributional cost of the status quo
Removes the 'brain drain' externality on ordinary schools' peer composition and incentive structure, even though this benefit is unquantified (F-live-education_finance-06)
threats: Political reversal risk (as in Poland post-2016) could waste sunk implementation costs if reform is not institutionally durable
High symbolic salience and opposition mobilization could raise the political-economy cost of implementation beyond direct fiscal cost
weaknesses: No proposal-level cost estimate exists for restructuring Hungary's middle-tier schooling to match the Polish comprehensive model
Teacher workforce demographics (aging, low pay) imply implementation costs for training and incentives not accounted for in the mechanism description
